pair.title: Over-editing refers to a model modifying code beyond what is necessary

story.title: Coding Models Are Doing Too Much

comments.text: I'm skeptical this is actually a widespread problem worth optimizing for. Every time I've used Claude or Copilot to fix a bug, it's been surgical. The examples I see online tend to be either (a) deliberately bad prompts like "rewrite this function to be better" or (

kids.text:
I tested this with Claude and Cursor on a real codebase last week. Asked Claude to fix a null-pointer bug in a 12-line validation function. It came back with 47 lines: added logging, restructured the whole thing with early returns, renamed variables, added a helper. The fix itself was correct but the diff was unreadable.

Then I tried the same prompt but added "make minimal changes, only fix the bug". That helped but not entirely. It still added one unnecessary guard clause.

The parent's right that prompts matter, but I don't think that's the whole story. These models seem to optimize for "clean code" as a side effect of their training, not

I ran the same experiment on our codebase with four different models. Claude 3.5 Sonnet added unnecessary null checks 73% of the time. GPT-4o was better at 42%. Gemini 2.0 was worst at 89%. The constraint prompt helped

comments.text: I wonder if this is a real problem in practice or if it's being overstated. Every time I've used Claude or Copilot to fix a bug, the edits have been pretty surgical. The examples shown here feel cherry-picked.

kids.text: I've actually had the opposite experience. Claude Code will sometimes add a null check or restructure a loop even when the bug fix is trivial. I switched to using aider (https://aider.chat/) which gives you more control over the scope of changes. It's designed specifically for surgical edits and

comments.text:
I've actually measured this with Claude and GPT-4 on a codebase I maintain. Asked them to fix 12 bugs (off-by-ones, logic errors, type mismatches). Claude stayed surgical on 10 of them, rewrote aggressively on 2. GPT-4 was closer to 7/12.

The thing is, it correlates hard with how specific your prompt is. "Fix the bug on line 47" gets you a 50-char

kids.text:
This reminds me of the broader problem with how we think about code ownership and authorship. When a model rewrites half your function, you're not just getting a code change-you're getting a loss of your own stylistic fingerprint, which matters more than people admit.

I've seen this play out differently in different contexts. In a startup where velocity matters and code review is light, over-editing barely registers as a problem. You merge it, move on. But in a mature codebase with institutional knowledge baked into naming conventions and structure, those "improvements" the model makes actually erase context that only exists in the code's shape. The variable name `remaining_retries_after_backoff` exists for a reason. It's not verbosity, it's documentation.

The other angle is that models are optimizing for a different objective than what you asked for. You asked for a bug fix. The model has learned that good code is refactored code, is well-structured code

I've been using Sourcegraph's Cody for about 6 months on a legacy Rails codebase with the same frustration. The surgical edits are there, but you're right that specificity in the prompt matters enormously. I started prefixing requests with "minimal change only" and the diff sizes dropped by maybe 40%. That said, Cody still rewrites more than Claude does on our machine-we actually switched to Claude for critical paths because of this exact issue. The code review tax wasn't worth the velocity gain. Worth trying if you're hitting this wall

I've seen this exact pattern. Asked Claude to refactor a search function that had a subtle off-by-one in the pagination logic. It fixed the bug correctly but also reorganized the entire loop structure, extracted a new helper method, and added caching I never asked for. The diff was 47

The real issue here is that we've framed this as a model behavior problem when it's actually a governance gap. If you're letting Claude or GPT-4 commit directly to your codebase, you need review gates. But that's expensive, which is why we're seeing pressure to train models to be "faithful editors" instead of fixing the actual workflow.

The better pattern: models generate diffs, humans review diffs, humans approve commits. That's not new. Version control exists for exactly this reason. The problem isn't that Claude over-edits, it's that

comments.text:
I've used Claude Code and Cursor extensively over the past year, and I actually haven't seen this as a major problem in practice. When I ask for a specific fix, both tools tend to be pretty surgical about it. The over-editing happens maybe 20% of the time, and usually only when the initial prompt is vague.

The real issue I've noticed is the opposite: models sometimes make minimal changes that don't actually address the root cause. Paul

kids.text: I think you're describing two different failure modes. Over-editing happens when you ask for a fix without context ("make this faster"). Minimal fixes happen when you ask for something surgical ("change this one line"). The model has to guess your intent, and it's biased toward safety and completeness when the intent

> The model has to guess your intent, and it's biased toward safety and completeness when the intent

This is just excuse-making. The actual problem is that these models have no concept of "minimal change" as a goal. They're trained on GitHub where refactoring and bug fixes get mixed together constantly. So when you ask them to fix code, they do what the training data told them: clean it up, rename things, add validation.

Paul's right that it depends on how you prompt. But that's the real issue - you have to treat these tools like unreliable contractors who need extremely specific instructions or they'll gold-plate everything. That's not "surgical

I think you're describing two different failure modes. The over-editing happens most when the model has to infer intent from context-like when you point at a bug and say "fix this" without explicitly stating the constraint "only change what's broken."

The inverse problem you mention (insufficient fixes) usually crops up with more constrained prompts. If you say "change X to Y," the model might do exactly that and miss that X was wrong in three places.

The real insight from the article seems to be that models can be trained to recognize edit scope. You can nudge them toward surgical fixes by

I ran into this exact thing last week with Cursor on a Django view. Asked it to add pagination to a queryset, and it rewrote the entire view, renamed variables, added a new utility function. The fix itself was correct but the diff was unmergeable without basically re-understanding the whole thing

comments.text: Reminds me of the old Perl motto "there's more than one way to do it" but inverted. Back when I used Vim + ctags for refactoring, you had to be surgical or risk breaking everything. Now models just... rewrite. I tested this with Claude on a simple

kids.text: This reminds me of the broader shift from editing to generation. With Vim and ctags, you had to understand the code deeply enough to make minimal changes. Now the model is incentivized to "complete" tasks by rewriting, which feels more thorough to it. Similar problem with writing: LL
